prompt: I have a match with three arms that all do the same thing but for different values, and I'm repeating the arm body.
error_message: No compiler error — but the repeated arms are verbose. Or-patterns collapse them.
explanation: Or-patterns (|) combine multiple patterns into a single match arm. The body executes for any of the listed patterns. matches!(value, pat1 | pat2 | ...) is the combinator form that returns bool. Or-patterns avoid repeating the same arm body across multiple values, making the intent (grouping equivalent cases) explicit.

prompt: I want to use or-patterns to match multiple enum variants that share the same handling logic.
error_message: No compiler error — but three separate arms with `true` can be an or-pattern.
explanation: Or-patterns with enum variants (Status::Active | Status::Pending) group variants with the same handling into a single arm. matches! is the idiomatic form when only a bool result is needed. The compiler still checks exhaustiveness — omitting any variant is a compile error.

prompt: I want to use or-patterns inside a let statement to destructure one of several possible shapes.
error_message: No compiler error — but the match can be collapsed with or-patterns that bind to the same name.
explanation: Or-patterns in a match arm must bind the same variable names in all alternatives. Shape::Circle { radius: val } | Shape::Square { side: val } both bind val — the field is renamed on the left side. If an or-pattern alternative doesn't bind a name that others do, the compiler reports E0408.

prompt: I'm getting E0408 because my or-pattern alternatives bind different variable names.
error_message: variable `code` is not bound in all patterns; variable `key` is not bound in all patterns
explanation: E0408: or-pattern alternatives must bind identical variable names. Event::KeyDown { key } | Event::KeyUp { code } is invalid because key exists only in the first alternative and code only in the second. Rename the field using the field_name: bind_name syntax: Event::KeyDown { key } | Event::KeyUp { key: key } — or simply ensure both structs use the same field name.

prompt: I want to use or-patterns in a while let or for loop guard to accept multiple pattern shapes.
error_message: No compiler error here — demonstrating or-patterns in while let.
explanation: Or-patterns also work in while let, for loops, if let, and function argument positions (all pattern contexts). In while let, the loop continues while any of the or-pattern alternatives match. Range patterns (0..=100) can be combined with @ bindings to capture the matched value.

prompt: I want to use or-patterns with range patterns to classify a numeric value into buckets.
error_message: No compiler error — but enumerating each value is absurd. Range patterns fix this.
explanation: Range patterns (90..=100) match any value in a closed range. They can be combined with or-patterns (range | range | literal) in the same arm. This is the standard way to classify numeric values and characters without enumerating every value. Ranges in patterns are always inclusive (..=).

prompt: I want to use or-patterns inside a nested struct pattern to match multiple inner values.
error_message: No compiler error — but the two arms with true can be one or-pattern.
explanation: Or-patterns can appear anywhere a pattern is valid, including inside struct patterns. Message { kind: MessageKind::Error | MessageKind::Warning, .. } matches a Message whose kind field is either Error or Warning. The .. ignores all other fields. matches! works the same way.

prompt: I want to use or-patterns in a let binding to destructure one of several tuple shapes.
error_message: No compiler error — demonstrating or-patterns in let and match with tuples.
explanation: Or-patterns work in tuple patterns too: (0, _) | (_, 0) matches any tuple where either component is zero. matches! with or-patterns is the ergonomic form when only a bool is needed. Pattern guards (if x == y) add additional conditions that or-patterns alone can't express.

prompt: I want to use or-patterns to handle errors from multiple sources the same way.
error_message: No compiler error — but Parse and OutOfRange share the same arm body. Or-pattern collapses them.
explanation: Or-patterns can mix unit variants (Overflow), tuple variants (Parse(_)), and struct variants as long as the bindings are consistent. AppError::Empty | AppError::Overflow both have no bindings, so they can be combined. AppError::Parse(_) uses a wildcard for the inner value when it's not needed in the arm body.

prompt: I want to use or-patterns with @ bindings to capture the matched value from one of several patterns.
error_message: No compiler error — but 1, 2, 3 arms can be combined with @ binding.
explanation: x @ (1 | 2 | 3) combines an @ binding with an or-pattern in parentheses. The @ binds the matched value to x while the parenthesised or-pattern specifies which values match. x @ 4..=10 similarly binds the range match to x. Parentheses are required around or-patterns used with @.

prompt: I want to use or-patterns inside function argument patterns to handle multiple input forms.
error_message: No compiler error — demonstrating or-patterns in function arguments and closures.
explanation: Or-patterns appear in any pattern context: function arguments, closure arguments, iterator filter/map callbacks, if let, while let. The matches! macro is the most convenient form for closures and conditions. Pattern guards (n @ 1..=100) combine binding with a range check in a single pattern.

prompt: I want to combine or-patterns with guards in the same match arm.
error_message: No compiler error — combining guards with or-patterns is slightly nuanced.
explanation: A pattern guard (if condition) in a match arm applies to the entire or-pattern — the arm fires only when any alternative matches AND the guard is true. matches!(n, pat1 | pat2 if guard) works the same way. Be careful: the guard cannot reference names bound in only one alternative of an or-pattern.

prompt: I want to use or-patterns to handle multiple Result variants with similar error handling.
error_message: No compiler error — Io and Parse can be grouped with or-pattern.
explanation: Or-patterns with mixed variant kinds (tuple variant ConfigError::Io(_) and struct variant ConfigError::Validation { .. }) work in the same arm, provided bindings are consistent. Since neither pattern binds any name in error_category, they can freely be combined with |.

prompt: I want to refactor a chain of if-else-if comparisons into a match with or-patterns.
error_message: No compiler error — but the if-else chain is a prime candidate for a match with tuple patterns and guards.
explanation: A chain of if-else-if conditions comparing the same values is the classic pattern to replace with match. Tuple patterns (r, g, b) capture all three components simultaneously. Or-patterns (255, 0, 0) | (0, 255, 0) | (0, 0, 255) group the primary colours in one expression. Pattern guards handle the grey case where a formula is needed.

prompt: I want to use or-patterns to handle keyboard shortcut events that trigger the same action.
error_message: No compiler error — but the first three arms for quit and three for confirm can each be collapsed.
explanation: Or-patterns naturally model keyboard shortcuts: several keys trigger the same action. Key::Char('q') | Key::Char('Q') | Key::Escape groups all quit-triggering keys in one readable arm. Note Key::Char('q' | 'Q') is also valid — or-patterns nest inside variant patterns. This maps directly to how a keyboard shortcut table is conceptually organised.

prompt: I want to use or-patterns with slice patterns to match arrays of multiple lengths.
error_message: No compiler error — showing or-patterns with slice patterns.
explanation: Slice patterns ([x, ..], [], [a, b]) can be combined with or-patterns. [1, ..] | [2, ..] matches any slice starting with 1 or 2. The rest pattern (..) matches zero or more remaining elements. Or-patterns with slices are particularly useful for protocol parsing where several message lengths trigger the same handler.

prompt: I want to understand how or-patterns interact with reference patterns.
error_message: No compiler error — but &0 | &1 | &2 | &3 can be simplified.
explanation: When matching a reference, Rust automatically dereferences the scrutinee to match the pattern. So match n (where n: &i32) with pattern 0 matches when *n == 0 — you don't need to write &0. Or-patterns work the same way with auto-deref. Matching *s with string literal patterns ("yes" | "y") is the idiomatic way to match &str slices.

prompt: I want to use or-patterns inside a for loop to process only elements matching certain criteria.
error_message: No compiler error — but the if-or chain is the classic candidate for matches!.
explanation: matches!(expr, pat1 | pat2 | ...) is idiomatic when you need a bool condition in an if or while. It replaces verbose ||  chains. In for loop bodies, a match with or-patterns groups tokens by kind cleanly. Pattern guards (t if t.starts_with('"')) handle cases not expressible as literal patterns.

prompt: I want to use or-patterns to simplify a parser that recognises multiple token forms.
error_message: No compiler error — four identical arms collapsible with or-pattern.
explanation: A token classifier with many variants is the textbook use case for or-patterns. matches! with a long or-pattern list is far more readable than a match with many identical true arms. Note that Token::Int(_) in matches! uses a wildcard to ignore the inner value — the pattern just checks the variant tag, not the payload.

prompt: I want to use or-patterns to handle multiple error types from different library functions in one arm.
error_message: No compiler error — showing how or-patterns compose for typed error matching.
explanation: Or-patterns shine in error handling: should_retry(e) groups retryable errors in one matches! call; error_code(e) groups errors by HTTP status code. The pattern MyError::Negative(_) | MyError::TooLarge(_) matches either variant while ignoring the contained value. This makes error categorisation code self-documenting.

prompt: I want to use or-patterns with a match on Option to handle Some of specific values the same way as None.
error_message: No compiler error — the first three skip-arms can be collapsed with an or-pattern.
explanation: Or-patterns work seamlessly across Option variants: None | Some(0) | Some(-1) handles None and specific Some values in the same arm. This is more readable than several identical arms. The combinators version (map_or) is an alternative when a bool is needed for further logic. The pair matching shows or-patterns in tuple positions.

prompt: I want to check exhaustiveness with or-patterns — does adding a new variant force me to update or-pattern arms?
error_message: No compiler error — demonstrating that exhaustiveness checking still applies to or-patterns.
explanation: The Rust compiler enforces exhaustiveness even with or-patterns. Adding Direction::NorthEast to the enum without covering it in the match is a non-exhaustive patterns error. Or-patterns don't weaken exhaustiveness checking — the match must still cover every variant, either individually, in or-groups, or with a wildcard. This makes refactoring safe.

prompt: I want to use or-patterns to simplify a state transition function that maps several states to the same next state.
error_message: No compiler error — but FlashingRed and FlashingYellow both return themselves and can be collapsed.
explanation: State machines often have groups of states that share the same transition rule. FlashingRed | FlashingYellow => self handles both flashing states returning to themselves in one arm. The return value self refers to the matched value regardless of which alternative fired — a key property when or-pattern arms return a bound variable.

prompt: I want to use or-patterns to match multiple string literal patterns in a command parser.
error_message: No compiler error — but the first six arms pair nicely into two or-groups.
explanation: String literal patterns with | are common in command parsers and configuration readers. run_command handles quit/exit/q as one group and help/h/? as another — the grouping directly documents that these are synonyms. parse_bool shows another typical use: mapping multiple string representations to the same typed value.

prompt: I want to use or-patterns inside a while let loop to process a stream until one of multiple sentinel values is seen.
error_message: No compiler error — but the loop/match can be a while let with or-pattern.
explanation: Or-patterns in loop conditions use matches! to check 'is the top value a sentinel'. The negated form (while matches!(v.last(), Some(n) if condition)) continues the loop while the condition holds and breaks when it doesn't. For event streams, matching Stop | Error in one arm and continuing on Pause documents the control flow clearly.
